title: Buffalo · The Rebound
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2005 + BPD reports 2006–2026 · 35 neighborhoods · real locations

hook.line: Buffalo crime, up sharply off its 2022 low
hook.sub: BPD reports · a one-year jump, 2022→2023 — then the map, up close

punchline.sub: +32% in one year under the same BPD measure · 2025: 12,352 — easing, still 44% below the 2007 peak

copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2005 the gray marks are the FBI's classic 'index' crimes. From 2006 the figures are BPD's own reported-crime records — Buffalo publishes only ten major crime types, a narrower net than most cities. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · BPD OPEN DATA
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly reports, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location, block-level, sampled.
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 35 neighborhoods — every dot a real reported location, anonymized to the block.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident's block — BPD rounds coordinates to roughly 100 m. 97.9% of records map to one of 35 neighborhoods — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2005) and BPD's ten major-crime types (2006+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Buffalo neighborhood reports the fewest major crimes?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Buffalo Police Department via data.buffalony.gov (public domain) · real block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 97.9% of records mapped to a neighborhood
copy.creditsSources: Sources: Buffalo Police Department crime incidents (2006–present) via data.buffalony.gov (U.S. public domain works, attribution BPD) & FBI UCR (1985–2005) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (ORI NY0140100). Neighborhoods: City of Buffalo official planning layer. BPD publishes only ten major crime types — no drug, weapon, or vice offenses — and rounds coordinates to the block (~3 decimals). Long-arc totals are citywide from the source; the map counts the 97.9% mapped to a neighborhood. A 2008 records-system gap appears as a real dip — disclosed, never patched. FBI UCR index crimes and BPD incidents are different measures, shown as two chapters and never equated. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 1992: the FBI-era peak — 33,699 index crimes reported.
2008: a false dip — five months sit near-empty in BPD's records system; shown as-is, never patched.
2022: 10,822 — the lowest year on record, less than half the 2007 peak.
2023: 14,319 — up 32% in a single year off the 2022 low.
2025: 12,352 — easing again, but still 14% above the 2022 low.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported incident location — anonymized to the block by BPD open data.
June 2023 — the busiest month of the last five years: 1,431 mapped reports.
North Park — Buffalo's busiest neighborhood for reported crime over the last five years.
Central Park: mapped reports up 79% since 2022 — the steepest neighborhood rise on the map.
The bounce, mapped: 9,979 (2022) → 13,303 (2023) — easing since, 12,145 in 2025.
